MC: What is the primary purpose of dimensionality reduction in machine learning?

column3: To remove features that have low variance from the dataset
To find a compact representation of the data that retains as much important variation as possible using fewer features
To normalize the numeric range of features before training
To select the most predictive features by comparing their correlation with the target variable

MC: MC
Why does redundancy between features motivate dimensionality reduction?

column3: When features overlap in what they measure, the distinct information they carry can be compressed into fewer dimensions without losing much
Redundant features cause the model to overfit because they introduce noise
Redundant features must be removed before scaling can be applied correctly
Redundant features cause distance calculations to return negative values

MC: MC
A correlation coefficient of 0.865 between total_phenols and flavanoids means which of the following?

column3: Total phenols and flavanoids are identical features that should be removed from the dataset
The two features have opposite effects on the model and one should be dropped
The correlation value of 0.865 indicates that flavanoids causes total phenols to increase
Wines with high total phenol content tend to have high flavanoid content, and the two features are measuring overlapping chemical properties

MC: MC
What does a correlation coefficient value near 0 between two features indicate?

column3: The two features are identical and one should be removed
The two features have a strong negative relationship
The two features have little linear relationship with each other
The two features are both uninformative and should be dropped from the dataset

MC: MC
What does PCA do to the original features when it transforms a dataset?

column3: It re-expresses each sample as a combination of new directions that capture the maximum variance in the data
It selects the most important original features and discards the rest
It scales each feature to have mean 0 and standard deviation 1
It removes correlated features and replaces them with their average

MC: MC
Which module in scikit-learn contains the PCA class?

column3: sklearn.preprocessing
sklearn.model_selection
sklearn.feature_selection
sklearn.decomposition

MC: MC
Why must data be scaled before applying PCA?

column3: PCA requires all values to be between 0 and 1 or it will produce an error
PCA finds directions of maximum variance, and unscaled features with larger ranges will dominate the variance calculation regardless of their actual importance
Scaling ensures that the principal components are perpendicular to each other
PCA cannot compute a correlation matrix on unscaled data

MC: MC
The fit-on-training rule applies to PCA the same way it applies to scalers. What does this mean in practice?

column3: PCA must be fit on the test set to ensure the principal components reflect unseen data
PCA must be fit on the full dataset before splitting to capture all variance
PCA is fit on the training data only, and the same components learned from training are used to transform both the training and test sets
PCA is fit and transformed in a single step on both sets simultaneously

MC: MC
In the Week 12 demo, PCA with n_components=2 transformed the training data from shape (133, 13) to shape (133, 2). What do the values in the two new columns represent?

column3: The values are the original feature values rescaled to a common range
The values are the correlation coefficients between the original features and the target variable
The values are the mean-centered versions of the two most important original features
Each value is the coordinate of that sample along a principal component, representing its position in the direction of maximum variance

MC: MC
What does the n_components parameter in PCA control?

column3: How many principal components are kept after the transformation, determining the number of dimensions in the output
The number of features in the original dataset that PCA will examine
The minimum explained variance ratio each component must capture to be retained
The number of iterations PCA uses to find the principal components

After fitting PCA with n_components=2 on the Wine dataset training data, PC1 explained 36.4% of variance and PC2 explained 18.6%. What does this mean?

column3: PC1 is 36.4% accurate and PC2 is 18.6% accurate when used for classification
36.4% of the wine samples are best described by PC1 and 18.6% are best described by PC2
Together the two components capture 55.0% of the total variation in the dataset, with the remaining 45.0% discarded
PC1 uses 36.4% of the original features and PC2 uses 18.6% of the original features

MC: MC
In the Week 12 demo, PCA() was used with no n_components argument. What does this produce?

column3: PCA raises an error because n_components is required
PCA keeps all components, producing the same number of dimensions as the original dataset
PCA keeps only the components that explain more than 10% of variance individually
PCA automatically selects the number of components needed to explain 95% of variance

In the Week 12 demo, kNN accuracy on 2 PCA components was 0.978, which was higher than the baseline accuracy of 0.956 on all 13 scaled features. What explains this result?

column3: PCA selects only the most predictive features and discards the least predictive ones
Using fewer dimensions always improves kNN accuracy because it reduces computation time
The discarded components captured mostly noise, and removing them made distance calculations more reliable
PCA reorders the features so that the most important ones are weighted more heavily by kNN

MC: MC
PCA is described as an unsupervised method. What does this mean in practice?

column3: PCA can only be used with unsupervised learning algorithms like clustering
PCA.fit takes only X_train and does not use y_train, finding structure in the feature data without class labels
PCA produces components that have no relationship to the target variable
PCA does not use the train/test split and is applied to the full dataset

MC: MC
How does PCA for visualization differ from PCA as preprocessing for a supervised model?

column3: PCA for visualization uses a different algorithm than PCA for preprocessing
PCA for visualization fits on the test set while PCA for preprocessing fits on the training set
PCA for visualization does not follow the fit-on-training rule because no model is being trained
PCA for visualization reduces to 2 components to create a scatter plot, while PCA for preprocessing reduces to a chosen number of components before training a model

MC: MC
What problem does NMF (Non-negative Matrix Factorization) solve that makes it an alternative to PCA in some situations?

column3: NMF can transform new data after fitting while PCA cannot
NMF captures more variance per component than PCA on the same dataset
NMF requires all feature values to be non-negative, making it appropriate for data where negative values have no meaning
NMF does not require scaling before application while PCA does

MC: MC
What is the key difference between t-SNE and PCA as dimensionality reduction methods?

column3: t-SNE cannot transform new data after fitting and is used only for visualization, while PCA can transform new data using the components learned from training
t-SNE finds directions of maximum variance while PCA finds directions of minimum variance
t-SNE requires scaled data while PCA does not
t-SNE produces more principal components than PCA on the same dataset
